title: Vehicle Storage and Permanent Storage Vaults
narrationScript: A sealed source spends most of its life in storage, and the rules there bind just as tightly as the rules on the job. Part 20 allows exactly two states: material not in storage needs constant surveillance, and material in storage must be secured against unauthorized removal or access. Every arrangement starts with the same baseline. Survey with a calibrated meter to confirm the source is shielded before you secure it. Lock the device and the container and pull the keys. Record it in the utilization log, and account for every source in the quarterly inventory. Storage containers may read no more than two hundred millirem per hour at the surface and ten at one meter. Vehicle storage adds Part 37: two independent tangible barriers plus a disabled vehicle — and removing the ignition key does not count. Permanent vaults add shielding sized to the public dose limits, a locked door posted Caution, Radioactive Materials, and a security zone open only to trusted, vetted people.

sections.heading: Storage Is a Regulated State, Not a Pause
sections.body:
This module covers what happens to sources between jobs — storage and shipment. This lesson takes the storage half: the truck that holds your camera overnight and the permanent vault back at the shop. Shipping is the next lesson.

Part 20 splits a source's life into exactly two regulated states, and you must always know which one you are in:

- **Not in storage** — 10 CFR 20.1802: the licensee must **control and maintain constant surveillance** of licensed material in a controlled or unrestricted area. Eyes on, able to intervene.
- **In storage** — 10 CFR 20.1801: the material must be **secured from unauthorized removal or access**. Locks and barriers replace your eyes.

There is no third state. A camera sitting on a tailgate while the crew eats lunch is neither surveilled nor secured — and that is precisely the condition in which sources are stolen and people are hurt.

10 CFR 34.3 defines a **storage area** as any location, facility, **or vehicle** used to store and secure an exposure device, storage container, or sealed source when it is not in use — locked or barriered against accidental exposure, tampering, or unauthorized removal. Note what that definition is not: a **permanent radiographic installation** is a shielded room or cell where radiography is *performed*; a storage vault is where sources *live between jobs*. Different purposes, different rules.

sections.heading: The Baseline: Survey, Lock, Log, Count
sections.body:
Four obligations apply to every storage arrangement, vehicle or vault, every single time.

- **Survey before securing.** 10 CFR 34.49 requires surveys with a **calibrated and operable** instrument — including a survey of the exposure device and the storage container to confirm the sealed source is in its **shielded position** before they are secured in storage. A camera with the pigtail parked an inch short of fully shielded looks identical to a safe one; the meter is the only proof you ever get.
- **Lock it, pull the key.** 10 CFR 34.23: exposure devices, storage containers, and source changers must be **kept locked — keyed locks with the key removed** — whenever they are not under the direct surveillance of a radiographer or a radiographer's assistant. A key left in the lock is, legally and practically, an unlocked device.
- **Verify the container's external levels.** 10 CFR 34.21 caps radiation from storage containers and source changers, with the source shielded, at **200 mrem/h (2 mSv/h) at any exterior surface** and **10 mrem/h (0.1 mSv/h) at 1 meter**.
- **Log and count.** The utilization log (10 CFR 34.71) tracks each sealed source — the device or container holding it, the radiographer assigned, the site of use, and the **dates removed from and returned to storage**. A **quarterly physical inventory** (10 CFR 34.29) accounts for every sealed source and every depleted-uranium device on the license, including where each one is located.

sections.caption: Compliant overnight vehicle storage: the locked camera is barrier one, the locked compartment bolted to the bed is barrier two, a concealed battery-disconnect disables the truck, and the perimeter survey shows public areas stay under 2 mrem in any one hour.

sections.heading: Vehicle Storage: Two Barriers and a Truck That Cannot Move
sections.body:
A radiography truck parked for the night is a storage area, and because a working Ir-192 source — commonly 50 to 150 curies when fresh — exceeds the **Category 2 threshold of 0.8 TBq (21.6 Ci)**, the physical-protection rules of **10 CFR Part 37** sit on top of Parts 20 and 34. For mobile devices, 10 CFR 37.53 demands two things whenever the material is not under your **direct control and constant surveillance**:

- **Two independent physical controls that form tangible barriers** (37.53(a)). The standard arrangement: the exposure device locked (barrier one) inside a locked steel compartment permanently bolted or welded to the vehicle (barrier two). A padlocked chain anchoring a locked device to the truck frame serves the same function. The barriers must be independent — one hasp, one lock, one box is one barrier, not two.
- **Disable the vehicle or trailer** (37.53(b)) — a concealed battery-disconnect, a starter or fuel kill switch, a wheel boot, or for trailers a hitch lock plus wheel immobilizer. The rule is explicit on one point: **removing the ignition key does not satisfy this requirement.** Thieves do not need your key.

Where you park matters as much as how you lock. The truck's surroundings are unrestricted areas, so the public dose limits of 10 CFR 20.1301 apply: **2 mrem (0.02 mSv) in any one hour** at any accessible spot and **100 mrem (1 mSv) in a year**. Survey the perimeter where people actually pass or sleep — the motel wall, the sidewalk, the next parking stall — and park away from occupied buildings when you can. Distance plus the device's own shielding normally keeps perimeter readings to a small fraction of the limits, and your documented survey is what proves it.

sections.title: Sources are stolen from trucks, not vaults
sections.body: Nearly every stolen radiography source in NRC event reports left the site in a stolen or burglarized **vehicle** — usually overnight or during a meal stop. Constant surveillance means direct control: eyes on the truck and able to intervene, not a glance through the diner window. If a device goes missing, treat it as an emergency. Notify your RSO immediately; the licensee must **immediately** telephone the NRC Operations Center (or the Agreement State) once the theft or loss is known (10 CFR 20.2201), and Part 37 adds immediate notification of local law enforcement for an actual or attempted theft. Recovery gets harder by the hour — and an exposure device in untrained hands is a lethal curiosity.

sections.caption: A permanent storage vault in plan view: walls sized so the adjacent unrestricted office stays within 10 CFR 20.1301 public limits, a locked steel door posted per 10 CFR 20.1902(e), intrusion detection inside a Part 37 security zone, and no flammables stored with the sources.

sections.heading: Permanent Vaults: Concrete, a Lock, and a Sign
sections.body:
A permanent vault trades the truck's mobility problem for a shielding-and-neighbors problem. The design logic runs in this order:

- **Shielding sized by the limits next door.** Walls, floor, and ceiling must hold every adjacent **unrestricted** area — the office behind the wall, the sidewalk outside, the floor above — within 10 CFR 20.1301: 2 mrem in any one hour and 100 mrem in a year. Verify with surveys, and re-survey whenever the stored inventory grows in number or activity; a vault adequate for two cameras is not automatically adequate for six.
- **Locked, always.** The vault stays locked except when a radiographer or assistant is present and watching (10 CFR 34.23, 20.1801), with keys controlled and issued only to authorized individuals.
- **Posted.** Radiography sources exceed 10 times their 10 CFR Part 20 Appendix C quantities by orders of magnitude, so the storage area must be posted **"CAUTION (or DANGER), RADIOACTIVE MATERIAL(S)"** (10 CFR 20.1902(e)). If levels in an accessible area could reach 5 mrem in an hour at 30 cm, radiation-area posting applies on top. Part 20 does allow a narrow exception for sealed-source rooms reading under 5 mrem/h at 30 cm from the container surface (10 CFR 20.1903) — but posting the vault is standard practice and commonly a license condition.
- **Housekeeping is safety equipment.** No fuels, flammables, or compressed-gas cylinders stored in or against the vault — a fire that reaches stored sources creates the emergency you studied in the fire lesson. And keep unexposed radiographic film well away: the low-level field around any vault will fog a film bin over weeks.

sections.heading: The Part 37 Layer: Security Zones and Trusted People
sections.body:
Parts 20 and 34 protect people from radiation; **10 CFR Part 37** protects the source from people. It applies to Category 1 and Category 2 quantities, set per radionuclide in Part 37 Appendix A. The thresholds that matter in radiography: **Ir-192 becomes Category 2 at 0.8 TBq (21.6 Ci)**, Co-60 at 0.3 TBq (8.1 Ci), Se-75 at 2 TBq (54 Ci). A fresh working source typically lands well above its threshold, and collocated sources **aggregate**: when breaching a single barrier would reach several sources, their activities are summed — so a vault full of Category 2 cameras can climb into Category 1 obligations.

For storage, the security program means:

- **Trustworthiness and reliability.** Unescorted access to Category 1 or 2 quantities is limited to individuals with a documented T&R determination, built on a background investigation that includes fingerprinting and an FBI criminal-history check (10 CFR Part 37, Subpart B). No T&R approval, no vault key.
- **Security zones** (37.47). The vault and the loaded truck are controlled-access zones, limited to approved individuals or escorted visitors.
- **Detection and assessment** (37.49). A documented means to detect unauthorized entry or removal — intrusion alarms with response, direct visual surveillance, or verification checks per the security plan — with that equipment maintained and tested (37.51).
- **Law-enforcement coordination** (37.45). The licensee coordinates with the local law-enforcement agency for response, and an actual or attempted theft triggers immediate LLEA and NRC notification.

Treat the security plan with the same respect as your operating procedures — and protect its details. Security information is not for the break-room wall.

sections.caption: The numbers that govern storage — commit these to memory.
rows: Maximum at any exterior surface of a storage container or source changer, source shielded
Maximum at 1 meter from a storage container or source changer
2 mrem (0.02 mSv) in any one hour
External dose ceiling in any unrestricted area — the parked truck's perimeter, the office beside the vault
Annual dose limit for individual members of the public
Category 2 threshold — Part 37 security applies (Co-60: 8.1 Ci; Se-75: 54 Ci)
2 independent tangible barriers + a disabled vehicle
Mobile devices not under direct control and constant surveillance
Physical inventory of all sealed sources and depleted-uranium devices

interactive.title: Storage Compliance Inspection
interactive.intro: It is 11 p.m. The crew has bedded a 90-curie Ir-192 camera down in the truck and walked to the motel. Four of these details describe a compliant overnight storage setup — one is a violation. Click the violation.

regions.label: Vehicle security: keys removed, crew has them
regions.feedback: Correct. With the crew at the motel, the camera is not under direct control and constant surveillance, so 10 CFR 37.53(b) requires the vehicle to be disabled — and the rule states outright that removing the ignition key does not count. A truck that starts with a screwdriver is a getaway vehicle with a gamma source aboard. Fix it tonight: throw the concealed battery-disconnect or kill switch, or boot a wheel, before anyone walks away.

regions.label: Camera locked and anchored inside the compartment
regions.feedback: Compliant. The camera is locked with the key pulled (10 CFR 34.23) and anchored inside the compartment — that is barrier one of the two independent controls required by 37.53(a). The violation is elsewhere.

regions.label: Compartment door padlocked
regions.feedback: Compliant. The locked steel compartment bolted to the bed is the second independent tangible barrier required by 37.53(a) — and with the key removed it satisfies 34.23 as well. The barriers are fine; ask what protects the whole truck.

regions.label: Radiation label on the compartment
regions.feedback: Compliant. The compartment carries the radiation symbol and DANGER — RADIOACTIVE wording, so anyone who encounters it is warned what is inside. Labels inform; they do not secure. Keep hunting.

regions.label: Perimeter survey reading at the fence
regions.feedback: Compliant. 0.5 mR/h at the nearest publicly accessible point is well under the 2 mrem in any one hour ceiling of 10 CFR 20.1301, and the documented reading proves the parking spot is acceptable. The problem here is not radiological — it is security.

checkQuestions.question: With the sealed source in its shielded position, 10 CFR 34.21 limits radiation levels from a storage container or source changer to:
options: 2 mrem/h at the surface and 0.2 mrem/h at 1 meter
50 mrem/h at the surface and 5 mrem/h at 2 meters
200 mrem/h at any exterior surface and 10 mrem/h at 1 meter
500 mrem/h at any exterior surface and 100 mrem/h at 1 meter
checkQuestions.explanation: 10 CFR 34.21 sets the limits for storage containers and source changers at 200 mrem/h (2 mSv/h) at any exterior surface and 10 mrem/h (0.1 mSv/h) at 1 meter, measured with the source shielded. Readings above these mean a shielding problem or a source that is not fully retracted — the container comes out of service.

checkQuestions.question: A crew leaves a Category 2 Ir-192 camera in the truck overnight. Which action satisfies the vehicle-disabling requirement of 10 CFR 37.53(b)?
options: Removing the ignition key and locking the cab
Switching off a concealed battery-disconnect so the truck cannot start
Parking nose-in against the shop wall
Leaving the two-way radio on so the crew can be reached
checkQuestions.explanation: 37.53(b) requires a method that actually disables the vehicle or trailer — a kill switch, battery disconnect, or wheel immobilizer. The rule states explicitly that removing the ignition key does not satisfy the requirement, and parking position or communications do nothing to stop a thief who can start the truck.

checkQuestions.question: Under 10 CFR 34.49, before an exposure device is secured in a storage area the radiographer must:
options: record the number of exposures made that day in the utilization log
wipe down the guide tube and connectors
verify the transport placards have been removed from the vehicle
survey the device and storage container with a calibrated, operable instrument to confirm the source is in its shielded position
checkQuestions.explanation: 10 CFR 34.49 ties surveys to storage: the device and container are surveyed with a calibrated, operable meter to prove the sealed source is fully shielded before they are secured. A source parked an inch short of shielded looks identical to a safe one — only the instrument can tell you which truck you are about to lock up.

checkQuestions.question: A permanent storage vault holding radiography sources must, at minimum, be posted with which sign under 10 CFR 20.1902(e)?
options: "CAUTION (or DANGER), RADIOACTIVE MATERIAL(S)"
"CAUTION, HIGH RADIATION AREA" regardless of dose rates
"GRAVE DANGER, VERY HIGH RADIATION AREA"
No sign — posting would advertise the sources to thieves
checkQuestions.explanation: Stored radiography sources exceed 10 times their Appendix C quantities by orders of magnitude, so 20.1902(e) requires the RADIOACTIVE MATERIAL(S) posting. Radiation-area or high-radiation-area signs are added only if dose rates in accessible areas actually reach those thresholds, and security concerns are handled by locks, barriers, and Part 37 measures — never by omitting a required posting.

checkQuestions.question: Which overnight arrangement provides the two independent physical controls required by 10 CFR 37.53(a)?
options: A locked camera sitting loose on the open truck bed
A locked camera inside a locked steel compartment that is bolted to the truck
An unlocked camera inside a locked cab with the windows up
A locked camera in a locked compartment with the key left in the hasp for the night shift
checkQuestions.explanation: Two independent tangible barriers must each resist unauthorized removal: the device's own lock is one, and the locked, permanently mounted compartment is the second. A loose device has only one barrier, an unlocked device wastes the first barrier, and a key left in the lock defeats the barrier entirely — and violates 34.23's key-removed rule besides.

references: 10 CFR Part 34 — Licenses for Industrial Radiography (§§34.21, 34.23, 34.29, 34.49, 34.71)
10 CFR Part 20 — Standards for Protection Against Radiation (§§20.1301, 20.1801–20.1802, 20.1902–20.1903, 20.2201)
10 CFR Part 37 — Physical Protection of Category 1 and Category 2 Quantities of Radioactive Material
ANSI/ASNT CP-105-2024, Radiographic Safety Operations Course §11.0–11.2 (storage of exposure devices and sources)
